title: Battling grubs, again
subtitle: and probably again and again
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
I did it again.

Last week, during one of those grey soaking rains we’ve been having, I moved a pot of brugmansia to check the drainage hole and something moved. Not one something. Many somethings. A pale, dense cluster of white grubs had gathered in the damp space beneath the container, tight and writhing, and I just stood there in the rain looking at them. I have never felt the word infestation quite so physically before.

That was just one pot. The medium-sized VegTrug I use for brassicas was worse — absolutely full of them, the soil practically animated when I turned it out. I tried hand removal first, which works fine until you have forty containers packed tightly together and roots threading through everything. At some point I accepted that I wasn’t going to pick my way out of this.

I am reluctant to write this, but I feel it’s best to grapple with the reality of container gardening AND local limitations on “safe” pest control. I used diazinon again this spring. I am not proud of this. There is no nematode option here, no envelope of predatory insects to order online, no “biological solution” waiting at Cainz. Japan has not made this easy for the person who would very much like to feel righteous about their balcony. I hate that I had to use a poison in my garden, and it was heartbreaking to remove all the flowering parts beforehand so as not to harm pollinators. But the scale of it had explained something I’d been puzzling over all winter: why things hadn’t quite picked up the way they usually do as the sun angle changes. The roots had been getting eaten alive while I was blaming the weather.

The effect was fast and, I’ll admit, satisfying in a way I felt conflicted about. The amount I used was almost implausibly small, like a sprinkling that wouldn’t be enough to salt a nice steak. Within a couple of days, dying grubs were surfacing everywhere. Within nineteen days, the chard and collards look stunning, fat and huge, and the snap peas have been almost too productive. The plants had simply been fighting an underground war I couldn’t see.

I want to address the obvious concern, because I had it too. The reassuring context, after some research, is that this is a soil incorporation treatment, not a foliar spray — and that distinction matters enormously for the vegetables I’m growing in these containers. Diazinon applied as granules into soil does not move efficiently into above-ground plant tissues. The 30-day harvest interval on the label exists primarily for root vegetables, where uptake through the roots is more direct, and for foliar applications, which are a different and higher-residue situation entirely. For eggplant, tomato, cucumber, pepper, all listed on the label, the margin is very wide. Good thing I used fresh soil for the potatoes anyway, though they’re nowhere near ready regardless.

Balcony Botany & Beyond is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

Japan’s Food Safety Commission (食品安全委員会) sets the ADI for diazinon at 0.001 mg/kg body weight/day, meaning a 70 kg adult’s safe daily threshold is 0.07 mg. At typical soil-application residue levels on fruiting vegetables, you would need to eat an implausible quantity of produce to approach that threshold within the PHI window.

I also felt guilt about the earthworms that had found their way into some containers, but I’ve come to think that’s misplaced sentiment. My raised fabric containers on a hard balcony surface are closed systems; worms that wander in have nowhere to go when things get too wet, too dry, or - and Tokyo does get very hot - simply too hostile. They tend not to survive long in this environment regardless. It’s fundamentally different from treating an in-ground bed.

The broader ecological guilt took a bit more talking myself down from. I’d been absorbing all the usual, entirely valid anxiety about collapsing insect populations. But these beetles, the big brown chafers you find everywhere in urban Japan, are one of the most common insects in the city. Parks, street trees, roadside planters, every patch of soil in Tokyo is full of them. Removing a few dozen from my containers makes no ecological dent. Their role in decomposing organic matter and feeding birds plays out perfectly well in every green space within a five-minute walk. In a container, they’re simply in the wrong place: a closed system with no natural predators, a finite root zone, and no capacity to absorb the damage. In open ground a plant might lose some roots to grubs and compensate; in a pot, a handful of third-instar larvae can consume the entire root mass before anything shows above ground.

I try to remember that the balcony itself is a net positive. Flowering plants feeding adult beetles, bees, pollinators. The odd grub I remove by hand, tossed over the railing onto the roof below, feeds a crow within about four minutes. I’m participating in the food web, just on my own terms, in an artificial system, making the judgment calls that system requires.

And my compost bin is still absolutely crawling with the buggers.

title: Spaced Out
subtitle: Spacing Is a Lie (And Other Things I've Learned By Ignoring the Packet)
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
I’m going to admit something up front: I have never read a plant spacing recommendation and thought, yes, that’s exactly what I’ll do. Not because I’m an anarchist about it, but because spacing as printed assumes something I don’t have — ground. Real, forgiving, extends-in-every-direction dirty ground.

What I have is basically a collection of pots. And pots change everything in relation to the traditional rules of gardening.

Revelling in spring, I’ve been reading Henry Mitchell lately. If you don’t know him, he was a garden columnist for the Washington Post for decades, and The Essential Earthman is the kind of book you keep opening to random pages because every page has something that makes you feel slightly called out and oddly reassured at the same time.

He wrote this, on the question of what to plant where:

“I don’t mean you have to know all about the soil and fertilizers and all that—a great deal of time is wasted by amateur gardeners playing chemist and pseudo-scientist... those gardens we admire are never the result of dumping and squirting: they are always the result of muddling things about in the brain and the eye.”

I’ve thought about that line a lot: Muddling things about in the brain and the eye. Not spreadsheets, not soil pH meters, or planning apps. Observation, and a willingness to fiddle. Though he clearly wrote more about in-ground gardening, I think he’d have had a lot to say about container spacing, because it’s one of the places where gardening advice is most aggressively prescriptive, and where the reality in the pot looks almost nothing like what the instructions promised.

What Spacing Advice Is Actually For

Spacing recommendations exist for a reason; they’re written for someone with a 10-meter vegetable bed who needs to know how far apart to plug in their brassica transplants to avoid crowding the roots and blocking air circulation. They assume even light from above, consistent soil nutrition, and infinite lateral expansion.

In containers, none of those assumptions hold. Your roots hit the pot wall, your soil dries out from every side at once, the light comes from one direction, or if you’re lucky two. The microclimate three centimeters from the rim is different from the microclimate in the center. Most critically — you are not managing ten square meters. You are managing a few 38-liter planters on a northwest-facing balcony in Tokyo, and the consequences of every decision are immediate and nowhere to hide.

What this means practically: official spacing is almost always too conservative for ornamentals, and quite often too generous for vegetables. You can push leafy greens surprisingly close together, thinning as you harvest, and they’ll be fine; meanwhile, a single tomato plant in a container that’s technically “big enough” will still behave like it owns the whole neighborhood, because in that container, it certainly does.

The Real Teacher Is the Pot Itself

Mitchell becomes useful again to me when he talks about the kind of gardener who, without any particular design philosophy, ends up with a lovely garden simply because they kept moving things that got in the way and kept the things that looked good. He calls it “pottering about,” and he means it as a compliment:

“Such a gardener, without even intending it, cannot help learning how big his plants get, and over the years he potters about, moving this plant or that because ‘it got in the way of the strawberries’ or some such thing.”

The beautiful thing about container gardening is that you can move things; that is, in a sense, the entire point. A plant in a pot is a plant on probation; it has to earn its spot on the balcony by performing, by not being a bully, by playing reasonably well with the plant next to it. If it doesn’t, I can pick it up and move it, or pot it separately. Or the nuclear option: I compost it and feel briefly painfully guilty and then much better.

This is a faster education than an in-ground garden. A bad spacing decision in a border might take a full season to reveal itself, but in a pot it is usually obvious within three weeks. The plant that is too close starts to reach, or bleach out, or just quietly give up, and the crowded pot that looked instantly full in April looks like a defeated mess by June — which is the classic container sin of planting too densely for a quick lush look and then watching it collapse under its own competition.

Balcony Botany & Beyond is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

The Decisions That Actually Matter

Mitchell again, because he puts it better than I can:

“He is either going to have his row of camellias or not — and if he does, then the other things he might just as easily have grown cannot be grown in that space.”

This is the real spacing conversation. Not centimeters between seedlings, but choices between whole categories of plant. My balcony is not small by Tokyo standards, but it is absolutely not large enough for everything I want to grow. The deep 38 liter planters that the tomatoes need are also the ones the eggplants want. And the VegTrugs have a finite square footage that I can calculate exactly but somehow always misremember when I’m standing in a garden center holding something I didn’t come in for, and cursing my circumstances.

I have far too many roses for any container gardener to reasonably handle. After their brief blooming is done, I wonder about the point of keeping my camellias.

Every time I commit space to an indeterminate tomato — and they always take up more than you think, always — I’m choosing not to grow something else there. That’s not a failure of planning; that’s the reality of the form. The question isn’t how to fit everything in. It’s which things matter enough to earn the space.

The answer, like Mitchell’s, is always going to be a bit personal, a bit irrational, and probably tilted heavily in favor of whatever I happen to be obsessed with right now.

What I Actually Do

Practically speaking, here is how I approach spacing in containers, after several seasons of getting it wrong in interesting ways:

• For fruiting vegetables — tomatoes, peppers, eggplant — one plant per large container, no exceptions. Roots are greedy, and they will fight to the death. After last year’s pepper mess, big showy veg get their own pots. Full stop.

• For leafy greens and herbs — I plant closer than recommended and treat the thinnings as the first harvest. A pot of mizuna sown densely and scissor-harvested at 10 cm is vastly more productive than the same pot with four plants spaced “correctly.”

• For ornamentals, I use the eye, not the ruler. If it looks full but not crowded, it probably is. If I have to squeeze it in, I wait until something else comes out.

• For anything new, I [should] start it in its own pot, observe it for a season [but I’m impatient], and make spacing decisions based on what I actually see rather than what the label says. The label was not written with my balcony in mind.

The deeper principle, which I learned more from embarrassing failures than from any book: spacing in containers is less about the specific measurement and more about understanding which plants are actually competing, and for what. Two basils crowded together compete mainly for light. Two peppers crammed in one pot will fight over water, root space, and nutrients simultaneously, and both will lose.

Mitchell ends his planting chapter with a small piece of permission that I find I need to reread occasionally:

“Nobody should be uneasy, then, or fearful to set out planting whatever he likes all over the place.”

Or perhaps Vita Sackville-West said it better; “cram, cram, cram, every chink and cranny.” It is, given everything I’ve just argued about making hard choices, maybe a strange note to end on. But I think he’s right. The anxiety about getting spacing exactly right, about making a plan and sticking to it, about having the “correct” number of plants per pot… that anxiety is the enemy. You plant, you watch, you move things around. You pot up what works and compost what doesn’t. You muddle things about in the brain and the eye.

The balcony will tell you, soon enough, what fits.

title: Losing Lilies
subtitle: A little lament...
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
Lilies are my better half’s favorite flower. Or is it roses? I’m tempted to aphorize that a good rose smells better than anything, but all lilies smell great; I’m afraid the latter might not be true. All those pretty daylilies smell like nothing, right? I’m on the precipice of a lilium rabbit hole, but let’s step back for a moment and focus on the good smellies. And if you’re one of those people who can’t stand the smell, I can understand your point of view in my brain, but I doubt we can be friends.

Last year was the first year I tried to grow lily bulbs in pots. I went a little crazy, as is my wont.

Subscribe now

It was one of those gardening leaps of faith, burying a weird looking thing in a container with the soil mixture recommended by whatever book or internet source I’d most recently seen. If you’ve never experienced it, a lily bulb looks like a bloomin’ onion of garlic cloves, soft and pulpy, like a slightly dried-out succulent. There’s no way something so fragrant and beautiful could come from a thing that moistly scaly.

Being a first-timer, I did a rather poor job of caring for my plants. I got some nice blooms, but the foliage and structure was pretty ugly. I did some light research on what to do to keep them safe after the foliage died back - but apparently I didn’t follow directions. Today, as Japan celebrates the spring equinox, I got to thinking I should be seeing shoots poking up, but all I saw across all the containers was leaf mulch. I decided to have a gentle dig to see what was going on.

Hooray! I found shoots starting to emerge… but… do you see that nasty criminal grub nestled under the bulb there? He was one of three villains that I tossed onto the roof for birdsnacks. Happily, this casablanca looks like we should have a productive summer, despite the intrusion. The other container didn’t fare so well; a mixture of smaller “mountain” lilies, most had sadly rotted.

But one tiny little bulb had good roots and a promising sprout. Searching through the bulb and sprouting characteristics I’m led to believe it’s one of the mountain lilies, which seems right because it’s much smaller than the new Casablanca bulb I bought this year.

So what have I learned? I’m not sure yet; I underwatered the early days of these plants, and then somehow let most of them rot over winter. I may resort to a bit of poison to keep the nasties out of these containers, because who could resist gnawing on a juicy bulb like that?

For now, I’ve repotted, conditioned their medium, and gathered them in a sheltered place where I can see them, reminding me to keep them watered until it’s time for real sun.

Someday, maybe I can grow lilies like my dad does:

title: Seedling Daze
subtitle: I started early, but it feels like there's not enough time or space
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
My desk is covered in seed packets. I put them away, organize, store… and then get an idea, or realize I didn’t plant something I had thought of earlier. I said recently I wasn’t going to write about seed starting, because that’s all over the internet… but it’s really one of my favorite things to do! I got so excited to see yesterday that my “Listada de Gandia” eggplants have finally popped up, I went on a bit of a seed-starting bender. I have these seedlings cluttering up the shelves I just built in the “guest bedroom”, leggy little things demanding space, and it’s March in Tokyo - warm days sneaking in, but that wind off the frosted glass still withers anything too tender. Container gardening means they’re not just “started”; they’re about to get bumped into VegTrugs and those 38L planters where the real drama starts, because pots don’t forgive the same mistakes as open ground.

The “Rules” of Starting Seeds

If you want advice on starting seeds, it’s everywhere. I tend to ignore a lot of it though, and I do OK. I guess my list of things to pay attention to:

  1. Sterile media; coir or something like that (I know our UK friends are firmly anti-peat, but I see all kinds of arguments pro and con, so I’ll stay out of it).

  2. Research if the seed needs light or dark to germinate; usually just a dusting of vermiculite on top will do the trick if its a small seed.

  3. Warm-weather plants need bottom heat; peppers especially show MUCH earlier if their little cells are kept warm.

  4. A window ledge is not bright enough [at least for long enough]; if you don’t believe me, find an app for your phone that measure light. I run grow lights 16 hours a day - they’re LED now, so cost almost nothing in electricity. This is the first year I’m doing this, and I’m doing it to get the head start because I know most everything except okra and eggplant will shut down around July when our horrible heat is here to stay.

  5. Harden off, thin out; I usually wait until I’m taking them out or potting up. If I have two healthy seedlings in a cell, I will “prick them out” and try to keep all viable plants.

Pricking Out and Potting Up

I have [finally] found a local source for interesting tomato seeds. Modern Japanese fresh-market tomatoes are dominated by hybrids like Momotaro, bred for medium size, pinkish color, sweetness, low acidity, and a refined umami flavor. Studies of Japanese tomato quality note that Momotaro-type cultivars are the main edible varieties in distribution, with newer hybrids (Reika, Saturn, Rinka 409, etc.) competing on sweetness, color, and flavor but still in the same general profile. Momotaro, for example, is an indeterminate hybrid that matures early, yields multiple harvests, and is widely regarded as the most popular fresh tomato in Japan. Garden centers and home‑center seed racks mainly carry these domestic F1 lines (Momotaro series, Aiko, Reika, etc.), so open‑pollinated heirlooms, especially foreign ones, are simply not in the standard wholesale catalogs and therefore rarely reach casual home gardeners.

Subscribe now

Because consumers and supermarkets expect that specific pink, sweet, low-acid, crack‑resistant salad tomato, there is less commercial incentive to stock big beefsteaks, green-when-ripe, black, or strongly acidic heirloom types that deviate too far from that standard.

Last year, in the “interesting” column I found locally Brandywine pink tomato seeds, and also beautiful speckled roman. The Brandywine did nothing - beautiful foliage, strong growth, some flowers, but NOT A SINGLE FRUIT. FOR SIX MONTHS. So I’m not wasting space on that again.

So this year I’m trying to be a bit more ruthless with the real estate. If a variety can’t be bothered to fruit in a city where the growing season runs frost‑free from late February into December, it doesn’t get a second chance on my balcony. The seed racks and glossy catalog photos are full of promise, but out here on a hot concrete slab that starts flirting with 30‑plus degrees and soup‑like humidity by July, promise has to meet physics. The tomatoes that stay are the ones that set early, shrug at the first heatwave, and keep producing when even I don’t want to be outside anymore. I have a feeling that’s going to be a short list, and I pray that I’ll be able to write down some I’ve started this year.

And that’s really why I keep starting so many seeds, even as I swear I’ll be “sensible” next year. Every little cell tray is an experiment: which tomatoes can handle Tokyo’s wet‑blanket summers, which peppers wake up fastest on the heat mat, which basil sulks the least when I forget to water for a day. Seed starting isn’t just about growing plants; it’s about quietly rewriting the cast list, one season at a time, until the balcony is full of things that have actually earned their place. The packets on my desk look chaotic, but they’re really just my running notes on what worked, what failed spectacularly (looking at you, Brandywine), and what might surprise me next.

title: Growing Grocery Ginger
subtitle: Instead of letting this one shrivel into compost, let's try growing!
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
It’s been a while since I posted anything here, but that’s really because a lot has been happening “behind the scenes”. I’ve started more seeds than I really have room to grow, and invested in a couple of grow lights and heat mats, but didn’t think documenting that here would be all that fruitful since there is so much out there on seed starting. However, in late January I started a ginger growing experiment, and began writing about it until I realized I had no results to report:

Poking at this knobbly ginger root that’s been sitting on my kitchen counter for a week now, those pale little nubs finally perking up like they know spring’s knocking. I grabbed it from the supermarket down the hill last month, meant for a stir-fry that never happened, and now half is sprouting while the other half waits in the fridge. But with Tokyo’s March still dipping to 6°C and below at night, I’m planting anyway. Let’s see what happens.

First, I put it in a bag with a damp paper towel to encourage it a bit. Weeks went by, and I really wasn’t sure of any progress; eventually I compared it to a newer purchase and noticed the white nubs or “eyes” were getting bigger. Then I threw it in a plastic tray with some soil and waited.

Fast-forward to today: I have one sprout! One lonely ginger shoot in the tray. Vivid green, barely poking up like it’s testing the air. This is apparently normal for now; store-bought rhizomes take their sweet time waking up, especially this early in March in Tokyo.

Subscribe now

Videos I watched show the same: first a lonely green spike, roots forming below before anything else happens. It takes two weeks sometimes, especially with treated store ginger. If it fattens up, I’ll top-dress with more mix as the stem grows, keep fertilizing light. No rush; ginger is an eight-month wait anyway, harvested in autumn when the leaves yellow and I can dig up whatever knobs have formed. Enough for tea, maybe a stir-fry, nothing that’ll fill the spice shelf but better than nothing.

Part of me wonders if it’ll even bulb up properly in a tray this small. Should I repot now into one of the 38L pots, give it room to spread? The peppers are crowding the shelf already, and soon the tomato seedings will be taking over like they own the place. But disturbing it this early might shock the roots. Nah, I’ll wait till the shoot hits 15cm or so, knocking against the grow light, then ease it into something bigger with fresh mix. In the meantime, it’s a reminder how most of this balcony game is just watching and waiting, reading clues from aboveground while the real action happens out of sight.

The photo shows it clearly enough: tray labeled “Ginger” in my sloppy handwriting, that single shoot dead center amid the dark soil speckled with perlite. Other trays around it have beans and peppers pushing faster, because of course they do - though those peppers started in January. This one’s playing the long game. If you’re reading this and eyeing your own supermarket ginger, pick pieces with eyes, soak if you want, plant shallow in well-draining mix, and keep it warmish and moist. Don’t expect fireworks right away. Mine has proved it’s alive, at least.

What do you all think, tough it out in the tray or bump it up now?

Leave a comment

title: How Plants Actually Survive Winter
subtitle: (And Why I’m Watching Mine Too Closely)
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
Or: what’s really happening under those snow-covered leaves

I filmed my balcony after last Sunday’s snow, and I’ll admit—I was out there a bit too quickly with my phone, checking on everything. The mizuna under a dusting of white. The garlic shoots still green and defiant. The supposedly-dormant perennials that I keep poking at to make sure they’re “okay.”

But watching them sit there, coated in snow and perfectly unbothered, got me wondering: what are they actually doing right now? How does a plant just... survive this?

The Antifreeze Trick

Turns out, plants are much better chemists than I am. As days get shorter in autumn, trees and perennials start converting their starches into sugars—basically creating their own antifreeze. The increased sugar concentration in their cellular fluids lowers the freezing point, the same way salt keeps roads from icing over. Except, you know, it’s sugar. And it’s self-produced. Sounds healthier than diabetes...

Some plants also pump water out of their cells and into the spaces between cells, where it can freeze safely without rupturing anything important. The cells shrink a bit, the membranes become more flexible, and the ice forms in places that won’t kill the plant. It’s elegant, really… if you’re into that sort of thing. Which I apparently am now.

Subscribe now

Dormancy Isn’t Sleeping

I used to think dormancy was just plants “going to sleep,” but it’s more like they’re running in extremely low-power mode. Growth slows to almost nothing. Metabolic activity drops (even with all that sugar hanging around). Resources get redirected to the roots or stored as those sugary compounds I mentioned.

Herbaceous perennials let their top growth die back completely, hiding underground in roots, bulbs, or rhizomes until spring. Meanwhile, trees and woody plants stay visibly alive but internal—membranes shift, fluids concentrate, everything tightens up against the cold.

It’s triggered by photoperiod and temperature changes, which means the plants knew winter was coming long before I did. They’ve been prepping since autumn. Meanwhile, I’m the one standing on my balcony in February, filming snow like it’s a surprise.

After the Coldest Night

We just had the coldest night of the year so far, and as far as I can tell everything is still standing. The garlic is fine. The overwintered kale looks unbothered. Even that one chocolate cosmos plant I couldn’t bring myself to dig up seems like it might pull through.

And today? The sun is out. The snow is melting. It looks, dare I say it, like spring is actually coming.

title: Winter Gardening is Mostly Waiting
subtitle: Or: why I do inadvisable things with seeds and cuttings when there’s nothing else to do
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
It’s mid-January, and I’m standing on my terrace in a coat, staring at containers that are mostly just dirt with some optimistic green bits poking through. The roof shadow has claimed everything until April. No direct sun, just this slightly overcast light, and I can see other rooftops in full sun.

This is winter gardening in Tokyo. Technically, I can grow things. Practically, I’m growing impatience.

I started pepper seeds two weeks ago indoors, under grow lights, on a heating mat – the whole setup. It’s mid-January. I know it’s too early; at least the rational part of my brain knows this. The seed packet (and traditional advice) says to start 8-10 weeks before last frost, and last frost here is technically late March at the earliest, so I should’ve waited until... well, now, actually. Late January. February, to be safe. We don’t even really get frost here, hardly at all - though there was snow in the big trug when we returned from a trip to Sedona on January 5.

But the seed packets were sitting there on my coffee table, and it was cold outside, and I was bored, and before I knew it I had dirt under my fingernails and tiny cells full of hope arranged under a grow light in my living room.

This is what winter does to gardeners. We make poor choices.

Last week I planted out chard seedlings on the terrace. Again: no direct sun until April. The logic here is shaky at best. Chard is hardy, sure. It can handle cold. But can it handle no sun? We’re about to find out. I check on them every couple of days, crouch down to see if they’ve grown even a millimeter. They grow very slowly; last year’s experiment flowered almost as soon as the weather warmed. It was a little funny to see such small plants send up seed! A good way to keep from giving money to seed companies, I guess. So these pretty colorful baby chards… they’re just... sitting there. Existing. Waiting for the sun to come back, same as me.

Yesterday I took lavender cuttings. This is particularly funny because I have a terrible track record with lavender. I always forget when to trim it. I water it too much or not enough – lavender has Opinions about water, and I can never seem to guess right. I’ve killed at least three lavender plants since moving to this terrace. I’ve lost count.

So naturally, when I saw the one surviving lavender looking a bit leggy, I thought: I should propagate this.

Not “I should figure out how to keep the original alive first.” Not “maybe lavender and I are just not meant to be.” No. I grabbed my pruning shears and took cuttings like someone who knows what he’s doing, dusted them with the probably unnecessary rooting hormone, and stuck them in a little pot with medium, and now they’re sitting on the same shelf as the pepper seedlings, under the same grow light, participating in the same experiment of “will this work or am I just keeping myself busy?”

Odds are not great. But that’s the thing about winter gardening: it’s not really about the plants. Or not entirely. It’s about having something to do when there’s nothing to do besides skiing or taking cold walks in the city. It’s about tricking yourself into thinking you’re being productive when really you’re just... fidgeting. Tending. Fussing. Checking soil moisture levels for the third time today even though nothing has changed since this morning.

Winter in the garden is 10% activity, 90% impatience.

Subscribe now

The books and websites have all these confident plans for winter: “plant garlic in November,” “mulch your beds,” “plan next season’s layout.” Great. I planted garlic. I don’t have beds to mulch. And I’ve already planned next season’s layout twice – once in December, once last week when jet lag wouldn’t let me sleep, and I started mentally sketching container arrangements at 2am - even though I will of course just stick seedlings and seeds wherever they fit, still ignoring my Planter subscription for some perverse reason.

For me, winter gardening is mostly staring at things and willing them to grow faster. It’s checking on seeds that haven’t germinated yet. It’s moving containers six inches to the left to catch an extra twenty minutes of weak winter sun. It’s taking cuttings from plants you can barely keep alive because at least it’s something.

I’m not complaining, exactly. This is just what it is. The garden slows down, so you slow down, except you can’t quite manage it, so you invent little projects that may or may not make any sense. You start seeds too early. You plant in shade. You propagate things you’ll probably kill.

The chard might surprise me. As mentioned, it’s happened before – plants that limp along all winter suddenly explode when spring hits, like they were just conserving energy; a couple of last winter’s collards are still growing like mini-trees. Or maybe they’ll just sit there in that twilight state until the sun comes back and then decide whether they want to participate in life, or give me yet more seed.

The peppers, assuming they all germinate, will need to live indoors under lights for at least two more months before it’s safe to move them outside. That’s a long time to babysit seedlings in my living room. They’ll get leggy. I’ll probably forget to rotate them. But by the time March rolls around and I’m sick of looking at them, I’ll also be so attached that I’ll fuss over hardening them off like they’re actual children. So far, only a single Anaheim has sprouted:

The lavender cuttings? Honestly, I give them a 30% chance. Maybe less. But I’ll check on them anyway, probably too often, and feel a ridiculous amount of pride if even one of them roots.

This is winter. Waiting and watching and doing small, slightly irrational things to convince yourself that spring is coming. Moving plants around. Starting seeds. Taking cuttings. Standing on the terrace in your coat, squinting at dirt.

It’s not exciting. It’s not productive in any measurable way. But it’s what you do when gardening is less about harvests and more about hope. Or boredom. Honestly, it’s hard to tell the difference sometimes.

The sun will come back in April. The roof shadow will retreat. The chard will either grow or it won’t. The peppers will move outside, probably too early because I’ll be impatient. And I’ll have learned nothing about waiting, because I’ll do all of this again next winter.

For now, though, I’m just standing here. Staring at dirt. Willing something to grow.

Leave a comment

So far, only a single Anaheim has sprouted:
The lavender cuttings? Honestly, I give them a 30% chance. Maybe less. But I’ll check on them anyway, probably too often, and feel a ridiculous amount of pride if even one of them roots.

This is winter. Waiting and watching and doing small, slightly irrational things to convince yourself that spring is coming. Moving plants around. Starting seeds. Taking cuttings. Standing on the terrace in your coat, squinting at dirt.

It’s not exciting. It’s not productive in any measurable way. But it’s what you do when gardening is less about harvests and more about hope. Or boredom. Honestly, it’s hard to tell the difference sometimes.

The sun will come back in April. The roof shadow will retreat. The chard will either grow or it won’t. The peppers will move outside, probably too early because I’ll be impatient. And I’ll have learned nothing about waiting, because I’ll do all of this again next winter.
For now, though, I’m just standing here. Staring at dirt. Willing something to grow.

title: Dirge for my Dahlias
subtitle: Tubers prove tricky
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
Of the dahlias I tried to grow from tubers last year, I have one that is still growing.

Back in spring I had a whole little fleet of them, lined up in plastic pots like promises: this will be the year. Big dinner-plate flowers, buckets of blooms… the kind of gorgeous, slightly ridiculous abundance that only dahlias can pull off. I fussed over label stakes and potting mix ratios, and the angle of the sun on the terrace. I read too many instructions. After interminable green foliage in profusion, I was so happy when a couple of them bloomed beautifully!

And then, one by one, they all died.

Some rotted quietly in the pots. Some sprouted, sulked, and gave up. A couple just…never appeared. I kept (unhelpfully) poking around in the soil like a hopeful raccoon, looking for signs of life, and finding only soft tubers and that sour, wet smell they get when you’ve loved them with a little too much water.

By midsummer I had mentally written them off. Clearly I was not a dahlia person. Too fussy. Too many rules. Better to stick with the forgiving crowd: shisos (really a weed here, popping up in every pot) and mints and chilies that don’t fall apart at the first hint of overwatering - or in our case here in Tokyo, overheating.

Then, late in the season, this one plant quietly decided to be alive. It popped up in a pot I had half-forgotten, a spindly little shoot pushing through the mulch like someone arriving very late to a party and pretending it was on purpose. I almost pulled it out, thinking it was a weed. The stem looked wrong for a tomato, wrong for anything I thought I had planted there.

And then I saw the leaves. That dahlia shape, like a pointy, overgrown parsley. I remember actually saying “Oh” out loud on the balcony. Not a shout of joy. More like a surprised exhale. Oh. You’re here.

I shuffled things around to make space for it. It grew very slowly at first. Every few days the stem thickened, the foliage filled out, that particular dahlia green, a little glossy and a little coarse at the same time. Then it was leafing out lushly; this was late summer, already tipping toward autumn, and the plant seemed to know it was behind schedule. It didn’t waste time.

When the nights finally cooled, I dragged the pot into the unheated greenhouse. “Greenhouse” still makes it sound more professional than it is. It’s a Yardistry Urban Greenhouse I wrestled together on the fifth‑floor terrace in early May, a 66‑percent‑off impulse win from Costco, all frosted walls and a view of other apartment blocks. It smells like potting soil and cedar, and sometimes whatever I forgot to rinse out of the watering can. On our rare rainy days it sounds like being inside a drum.

The dahlia settled onto the floor, next to general debris (I really need to clean and organize that space). I told myself I was just giving it a bit of protection until the first frost. The truth is I wanted to see what it would do.

It kept going.

New shoots. More height. Little side branches. It was the most alive-looking thing in there, compared to the overwintering bare-stalked pepper plants, while everything else was slowing down and sulking into winter. Even the roses out in full sun had the decency to look tired - probably because I forgot their fall feeding. The dahlia, though… fresh leaves, a bright green stem, like spring had gotten lost and wandered into December.

Then one day, there it was: a flower bud.

Not big, not dramatic. Just a rounded lump with the faintest hint of color at the seam. The greenhouse was cold enough that I could see my breath some mornings, and yet this plant was putting energy into a flower. It felt reckless. Slightly inappropriate. I loved it instantly.

Every time I checked the greenhouse past, I gave the bud a quick inspection. Still small, but a little bigger now. The outer petals separating. Almost there. I imagined this ridiculous out-of-season bloom opening against a background of condensation and grey sky, this small, stubborn refusal to follow the calendar.

And then winter did what winter does. The bud stalled. It browned off, like it had changed its mind halfway through. It stayed in that “nearly open” stage for days, then weeks, and I could feel the plant shifting gears, slowly, from growth to survival. Leaves thickened, lost their shine. The whole thing seemed to hunch down a little, preparing.

While I was fussing over it, I noticed a lump half-buried in the mulch at the top of the pot. A tuber, somehow pushed up and exposed.

Balcony Botany & Beyond is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

I fished it out. It was small, tapered, like a pale, wrinkled radish with a long tail. Mostly firm, just a little suspicious at one end. I turned it over in my hand, brushing off bits of soil. It felt like something that might still be alive, or at least recently alive. There was that gardener’s question humming at the back of my mind: can this be saved?

On the table inside, under the desk light, I looked closer. Was that a neck? A crown? One of those mysterious “eyes” everyone talks about, the tiny point where next year’s plant is supposed to begin?

I squinted at it the way you squint at clouds, trying to see a shape. Maybe that bump. Or that one. It was like trying to read a language I’d only just started learning, one that mostly consists of the words “too late” and “maybe next year.”

In the end I did what gardeners do when they’re not sure: I reached for a knife.

The knife folded open resentfully from lack of attention and oiling, the blade already stained from other plant surgeries. I sliced the tuber cleanly across the middle. The sound was more carrot than potato - crisp, not soft. For a second I felt hopeful.

Then I looked at the cut surface.

The rim was pale and solid, but the center was hollowed out, a small brown cave in the middle of what should have been dense flesh. It wasn’t liquid or smelly, not dramatic rot, just an emptiness that didn’t belong there. A kind of quiet collapse from the inside out.

I made another slice, and then another, just to be sure. Same thing. A ring of living tissue around a missing heart.

It was weirdly affecting. I hadn’t named this tuber. It wasn’t a pet. But there was something about seeing the story of its year written in that cross-section - the start full of promise, the slow drying or disease, the way it had kept its shape while losing its substance.

It also confirmed what I already suspected: even if this piece had somehow managed to push out a sprout, it wouldn’t have had much to run on. The pantry was empty; whatever food it had stored for the future had already been spent.

The living dahlia in the pot, the one still throwing leaves and half-formed flowers in December, was probably feeding off other, healthier tubers below the soil line. This one, the stray I’d found on top, was just a leftover.

I left the pieces on the wooden tray for a while, partly because I didn’t feel like throwing them out right away, partly because I liked the way they looked next to the knife - small pale moons with dark centers. Autopsy photos for plant people.

Eventually I swept them into the compost, which is its own kind of mercy. They’ll turn into something else, feed some other experiment. Maybe next year’s mistake. Maybe next year’s unexpected miracle.

Back in the greenhouse, the dahlia is still there on the floor, thicker now, but a little more tired. I’ve started watering less, letting the surface dry out between visits. Another bud is probably never going to open all the way, and that’s fine. It tried. That feels like enough.

What surprises me most is how much I care about this one plant, this single survivor out of the half-dozen I managed to kill. It’s not particularly rare. I bought it from a regular garden center, a plastic bag with a photo that probably oversold the color. I don’t even remember the variety name anymore.

But I know exactly where it sits on the greenhouse floor. I know the way its leaves catch the weak winter light. I know how the stem feels when I brace it gently with one hand and check the soil with the other.

There’s a good chance I’ll mess something else up in the months ahead. Overdry it. Get cocky on the first warm day and move it outside too soon. Dahlias are unforgiving like that.

Still, I’m going to try to carry this one through the winter. Let it slowly go to sleep, not rushed, not forced. Just a gradual loosening of its grip on the season. In spring, if things go well, I’ll knock the pot out and see what’s underneath - hopefully new tubers, plump and solid, ready to start over.

And if there are none? If the crown has given up, if everything below the soil line looks like the hollow tuber on that tray?

A couple of months ago, after saying over and over again “no more dahlias! Why waste time on a flower with no scent!?!” I ended up buying a smaller guy that has survived nicely and has a nice fat bud waiting to open:

After traveling for Christmas and the new year holiday, I got back to a beautiful flower smiling away in the greenhouse. Stupidly, I didn’t take a photo then, but just now, almost three weeks after that photo, the fading flower is still gorgeous to me:

Here’s hoping I have more to share with you soon!

Leave a comment

title: Late to the Garlic Party, but Planting Anyway Because Why Not?
subtitle: Well, not really late... I've got plenty chugging away already!
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
I found three heads of seed garlic rattling around my little project closet today. Almost forgot about them completely, though they’ve been nagging at my brain faintly since I got the other garlic squeezed in where I could fit it, all over the place. Tired of seeing their fading foliage, today I pulled up the gladiolus corms from two of my rooftop planters, and suddenly there’s space. Perfect for garlic, right? Except it’s December 22nd. Way past prime time. But I’m planting them anyway. Let’s see what happens.

Garlic’s one of those crops that feels straightforward until you start digging into it. Or rather, planting it. You break apart a bulb into individual cloves, pointy end up, and shove them into decent soil about 5 cm deep. That’s the basics. Space them 15 cm apart if you want bulbs big enough to bother with. Closer, like 10 cm, and you’ll get more of them, but they’ll be puny. I’ve done both in my VegTrugs up here on the fifth floor in Nishiazabu. The optimal spacing leaves room for companions—lettuce or radishes that bolt out quick and get harvested before the garlic bulks up. No point wasting terrace real estate.

Why Fall’s the Sweet Spot (That I Missed for These)

Most places, including Tokyo’s mild winters, you plant garlic in fall. Late October here, maybe early November. The soil’s still warm enough—around 10-13°C—for roots to get going before the cold hits. They need that root establishment, obviously. Then winter vernalizes them, tricks them into thinking they’ve had a proper chill, so they bolt up and swell come spring. Plant too late, like now, and roots might not form in time. Bulbs stay small or rot if the wet seeps in. I’ve got my earlier batches—red garlic and elephant—from September and October. Those are hunkered down in so many different containers. They’ll be fine, even if their foliage isn’t too pretty. These stragglers? Jury’s out.

But here’s the thing: garlic’s tough. It’s not some delicate salad green. Young plants can handle freezes down to -10°C or so with mulch. Tokyo rarely dips that low—our zone’s more like 9b, with rare snow and occasionally rainy cold. My containers drain well, mostly coir-based soil and compost, so waterlogging is less of a worry. Still, December’s pushing it… soil temp’s are probably hovering at 8°C max up here, exposed but sheltered by that 1.5m glass wall. Roots will want to grow now, but the cold’s slamming the brakes.

The Experiment: Seven Cloves, No Expectations

So yeah, I’m doing it. I prepared the soil in the old 38 liter planter where the glads were. I’ll poke holes 5 cm deep, and dropped the cloves in. I should mulch but can’t decide between coir chunks (“Incredimulch”) or leaf mold. I’ll water them lightly, and if they sprout roots before January, great. If not, maybe they’ll sit dormant and wake up late. Worst case, I get scapes in summer—those curly flower stems you chop for pesto. Or tiny bulbs. Or nothing. But seven cloves take up about 30x30 cm, so no big loss.

This is balcony gardening for you. Space is tight—I’ve got the large VegTrug stuffed with brassicas and roots, herb one with dead basils and sulky sage, mint and tarragon sleeping for a few months. About eight 38L containers with fruit trees and roses, the strawberry tower still kicking out a few berries. Garlic fits the cracks. And honestly, experimenting beats regretting. Last year I messed up spring garlic because I forgot to cut the flowers off before they fully bloomed. This year’s fall crop is already promising, with fat shoots poking through mulch. These late ones I will just treat as a free data point.

Garlic Basics, No Fluff

Pick good stock. Firm bulbs, no mold, cloves plump. Elephant garlic is milder, bigger cloves, less punchy than the red or purple stripe varieties I favor. Plant pointy side up. Loose, well-drained soil, pH 6-7. They need full sun, but will tolerate the partial shade from my other containers come spring. Fertilize lightly in fall if you remember (I never seem to), then again when shoots hit 15 cm in March. Water steadily but not so it’s soggy. Hopefully Tokyo winter rains will handle most of it.

Harvest time is when half the leaves yellow, in mid-summer. Cure full heads, unwashed, in shade. And that should lasts forever. Pests? Few. Onion maggots stay away, and it repels aphids from neighbors. That’s why I tuck it near carrots sometimes. Downside: it ties up space for nine months. But one bulb yields 8-12 cloves. I’m not good at math but I like that multiplication.

Balcony Botany & Beyond is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

Tokyo Rooftop Twists

Up here at 35.6587°N, 139.7255°E, winters are damp more than deadly. The average low is 5°C in January, but wind whips across Minato. That frosted glass saves me, cutting exposure by half. Containers warm faster than ground, but dry out too. I try to remember to check the soil weekly. For these cloves, I’ll watch for rot. If we get a warm spell, the roots might catch up.

Spring planting is always an option. I’ll fridge the other fatter cloves for 4-6 weeks at 4°C to fake vernalization, then plant in late February when soil hits 10°C. Planting then will probably yield smaller bulbs, sure, but should be fine for pesto or roasting.

What Could Go Wrong (And Probably Will)

These seven? Odds are meh. Too late for full roots, maybe scrawny bulbs or just greens. Or they rot in a wet cold snap. The glads left the soil loose, though, which helps a lot. If they make it, bonus. If not, space frees up for more peas in March. Gardening’s half luck, half stubbornness. I could’ve chucked them, but didn’t. We’ll see how it goes!

I’ve planted plenty already this year. Red garlic’s my new workhorse—hot, dependable, stores without complaint. The elephant is for slower, sweeter roasts; I still remember a French-ish restaurant in Chicago serving whole roasted cloves to spread on bread. The September starts are grown up high now, green swords cutting the skyline. Even when the roses sulk, the terrace earns its keep.Readers, are you trying late garlic? Fridge trick or straight in? Comment below.

Leave a comment

Meanwhile, I’ll report back in spring. Bet on greens, not bulbs.

If they make it, bonus. If not, space frees up for more peas in March. Gardening’s half luck, half stubbornness. I could’ve chucked them, but didn’t. We’ll see how it goes!

I’ve planted plenty already this year.Red garlic’s my new workhorse—hot, dependable, stores without complaint. The elephant is for slower, sweeter roasts; I still remember a French-ish restaurant in Chicago serving whole roasted cloves to spread on bread. The September starts are grown up high now, green swords cutting the skyline. Even when the roses sulk, the terrace earns its keep.Readers, are you trying late garlic? Fridge trick or straight in? Comment below.
Meanwhile, I’ll report back in spring. Bet on greens, not bulbs.

title: Irrigation Irritation
subtitle: Container gardening is a lot different than dirt farming
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
This will be a long one today, folks… I’m doing an American Horticultural Society course on Ecological Gardening. The current module I’m on is “Prevention & Conservation.” If you garden in containers, “Dealing with Drought” by Nan Sterman in The American Gardener is a great article that… mostly isn’t talking to you. It’s written for people with actual ground, and a lot of its best ideas either don’t translate to containers or go sideways once there’s a drainage hole involved.

Here’s what I’d push back on from a balcony / container point of view.

Big irrigation systems for tiny pots

A huge chunk of the article is about “efficient irrigation technology”: in‑line drip tubing, soaker hoses, and smart controllers that run multiple zones on custom schedules.

That’s fantastic if you have a yard.

On a balcony with nine pots and a suspicious landlord? Not so much.

• In‑line drip tubing is designed to wet a broad root zone in the ground, not a 12‑inch pot. The emitters are spaced every 6–12 inches to create an even blanket of moisture in a planting bed. In a container, that turns into one awkward loop across the soil surface that you’re constantly bumping, tangling, and cursing at. I should know, I tried it.

• Soaker hoses are meant to snake through borders, not coil like a garden python inside a 16‑inch planter. They also release more water at the front than the end and clog easily in hard water. In a pot, that just means “muddy side / dry side” in a very small space.

Most container gardeners do better with:

• A watering can or hose wand and a 2‑minute daily check‑in in hot weather.

• If you really want automation, a simple battery timer plus drip stakes/tubing sized for pots—very different hardware than what’s described in the article. It’s what I’ve ended up with after much trial, error, and tears.

The whole irrigation‑zones‑and‑controllers universe is overkill for a balcony. It assumes you can install permanent hardware and that plants stay in one place for years. Containers move. A lot.

Balcony Botany & Beyond is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

“Deep and infrequent” watering… is how you kill pots

The article repeats the classic advice: water “deeply and infrequently” and wait until the soil dries “a couple inches” below the surface before watering again. That makes sense in real soil, where you have several feet of depth to work with and water can bank down below.

Containers are basically the opposite:

• Same root depth as in the ground, but with the sides and bottom exposed to hot air and wind.

• Way less thermal mass.

• Potting mix that’s often fast‑draining and peat or coir‑based (the brits just go ballistic about peat, don’t they?).

If you wait until the top couple of inches are dry in a 10–12 inch pot in full sun, there’s a decent chance the entire root ball is dry, especially in summer. Once peat‑heavy mixes go bone dry, the water you pour in tends to race down the edges and out the bottom, leaving a hydrophobic clump in the middle. I nearly killed my precious Meyer lemon tree this summer, letting the poor thing dry out too much. I had to soak the whole thing in a tub overnight to get him to drink again!

“Deep and infrequent” in a bed = strong roots.

“Deep and infrequent” in a black plastic pot on concrete in August = crispy basil. Delicious, but not productive.

With containers, the drought‑resilient pattern is usually:

• Water thoroughly (until it runs out the drainage hole).

• Check often, water again before the mix fully dries—sometimes daily or twice daily in heat, especially for small pots.

The principle of avoiding constant shallow sips still holds. The timing doesn’t.

Hydrozones don’t work when everything shares one pot

Sterman suggests grouping plants by water needs into “hydrozones” so each zone can be irrigated differently. That’s smart design for a yard: put the thirsty stuff near the hose, keep the tough natives on the far side, etc.

But in containers, we do this very non‑textbook thing: we plant a sun annual, a trailing plant, a filler grass, and maybe a herb, all in the same pot. Their water needs are not the same. Their roots are literally touching.

You can’t:

• Put your “high water” plants on one irrigation zone.

• Your “low water” plants on another.

They’re in one shared soil volume. One watering schedule. One fate.

For container gardeners, the real version of “hydrozoning” is:

• Don’t mix plants with wildly different water needs in the same pot (looking at you, rosemary + petunias).

• Group pots with similar needs together, so when you grab the watering can you’re not trying to remember, “Wait, is this the drought‑tolerant corner or the needy corner?”

Nice idea in the article, but the implementation looks very different on a balcony.

Soil microbiomes and the fantasy of permanence

The article spends time on “cultivating the soil microbiome,” saying not to till, just top‑dress with compost and let worms and soil organisms pull it down over time. It’s a very in‑ground, long‑term view of soil: you’re stewarding the same earth for years or decades.

Container mixes live shorter, messier lives:

• You often start with a sterile or soilless potting mix, such as peat or coir, perlite, bark, rather than actual mineral soil.

• You might dump and refresh that mix every 1–3 years as roots fill the pot and the structure collapses.

There is a microbiome in containers, especially if you reuse mix and add compost, but that “don’t disturb, just mulch and wait for worms to incorporate it over months” model is optimistic when the whole pot might be taken apart next spring.

For containers, what helps more is:

• Mixing in compost or worm castings thoroughly at planting time.

• Maybe doing light top‑dressing during the season.

• Accepting that this is more crop rotation than forever‑soil.

Same underlying idea (healthy biology = better drought resilience), but the article’s practice assumes an actual ground that isn’t going to be dumped in a wheelbarrow every other year.

Catching and storing rain… that you don’t actually get

Sterman talks about “catch and hold” strategies: swales, dips, permeable paving, banking water in the soil, and collecting roof runoff into cisterns to irrigate between storms.

In a yard, that’s smart. In a third‑floor walk‑up with east‑facing railings:

• You can’t dig swales.

• You don’t control the roof.

• Your landlord has Very Strong Feelings about anything that looks like “water storage.”

A few specific misfits:

• Using contours and swales to hold water on site makes sense at ground level; on a balcony, standing water is a structural and safety issue, and usually explicitly forbidden. And don’t forget mosquitoes…

• Big cistern systems are heavy. Water is about 8.3 pounds per gallon. A 100‑gallon tank is ~830 pounds before you add the tank itself. Most balconies are not signing up for that.

For container people, “catch and hold” usually looks more like:

• One modest rain barrel if your building and climate allow, or

• A bucket under the one place your gutter regularly overflows, or

• Just accepting that you, personally, are the irrigation system.

The concept—use free rain first—is great. The earthworks aren’t.

Mulch: 3–4 inches is way too much in a pot

Oh, mulch. Everyone talks about you so much, ranting and raving, but I still am just not that into you. The article recommends a 3–4 inch layer of mulch over the soil to suppress weeds, buffer temperature, and slow evaporation. In beds, that’s standard and helpful.

In containers, a mulch blanket that thick can cause problems:

• It eats into already limited root space in small pots.

• It can bury the crown of low‑growing plants and hold moisture against stems.

• It makes it harder to judge actual soil moisture without constantly digging around.

For pots, especially small to medium ones, a 0.5–1 inch layer of fine bark, shredded leaves, or even a light gravel top is usually enough to slow evaporation without suffocating everything.

The article also mentions inorganic mulches like rock and decomposed granite, especially for succulents, noting that water condenses on rock surfaces and trickles down. That is true in the ground. In a small container on a hot, reflective surface, a thick layer of dark rock can turn the soil into an oven.

If you’ve ever picked up a little black plastic pot with black rock mulch after 3 pm in July, you know that feeling. Your plant knows it too.

The subtle “no fertilizer” message

One more thing that’s easy to mis‑apply: the piece suggests you “don’t fertilize” because fertilizer pushes faster growth, which needs more water. That’s fair for established, in‑ground drought‑resilient plantings where the goal is stability and low inputs.

Containers are artificially cramped and watered more often; nutrients wash out faster. Most potting mixes don’t have long‑term nutrition built in. If you strictly “just say no” to fertilizer in pots, you often end up with:

• Hungry, pale plants.

• Stressed roots that are actually less drought‑tolerant.

Container‑friendly version:

• Skip heavy, high‑nitrogen blasts.

• Use gentle, slow‑release or diluted liquid feeds.

• Aim for “not starving” rather than “lush jungle.”

The core idea, don’t force plants to grow faster than your water budget can support, is still solid. It just needs adjusting when the plant’s entire universe is 14 inches wide.

So yes, “Dealing with Drought” is useful, thoughtful, and written from real experience. It’s just speaking ground‑garden. If you’re gardening in containers, you almost have to mentally run each tip through a “but what if this plant lived in a bucket” filter.

Different world. Same sun. Same dry spells. Very different physics.

title: Perennial Peppers!?!
subtitle: Possibly perpetual pretty peppers..
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
I came across a tidbit somewhere suggesting peppers are perennial. I’ve certainly always killed the plants in the past, so I was skeptical, and went on a deep-dive expedition to see how (and if) it’s done. Turns out: you absolutely can overwinter pepper plants in containers on a Tokyo balcony, and it’s a lot less glamorous than Instagram might suggest. It’s mostly: hack them back, disturb their roots, then ignore them for months.

The moment you realize peppers aren’t annuals

Early this month, my peppers looked done for: leaves yellowing, fruits wrinkling, one dramatic plant draped itself over the edge of the planter like it was fainting.

At first, I thought they needed more frequent watering, but noticed that water was just running out, and the moisture meter said they weren’t dry. At this point, a normal gardener would say “thanks for the harvest” and compost the lot. Instead, I dragged the whole container indoors and went down the overwintering rabbit hole.

Here’s the key discovery: most chilies and other Capsicum annuum types aren’t true annuals at all. They’re frost tender perennials, perfectly capable of living for several years if you keep them above freezing and give them a winter rest. That means earlier flowers, earlier fruit, and much bigger plants the following season.

Step one: brutal haircut

The first job is to forget being gentle. Once you’ve picked the last peppers, you cut the plants back hard. Really hard. Most guides suggest taking a 60–70 cm plant down to a 15–25 cm framework with just a few sturdy branches and several visible nodes.

In my case, that meant turning the wild tangle in the planter into something that looked suspiciously like a dead bonsai. I kept three or four strong, low stems and removed everything else: thin whips, crossing branches, all the leaves, every flower bud. It feels wrong the first time you do it. A week later, when there are no rotting leaves on the soil and no aphids marching around (so many aphids this year, everywhere – and STILL), it feels brilliant.

Step two: dealing with the root brick

When I finally slid the root mass out of the planter, it came out as a single solid block of roots and tired potting mix. Think doormat made of angel hair pasta. Being this root bound is actually a good sign: the plants were vigorous and working hard for you all season. But it’s not how you want to carry them through winter.

Most experienced pepper people recommend root pruning at this stage. You slice off roughly the outer quarter of the root ball all the way around, including a bit from the bottom, then gently tease some of the remaining roots loose. The goal is a smaller, tidier root system that fits comfortably in a pot about 2–4 L in size, rather than a huge planter that takes forever to dry out. In my case, the entire container was roots, with very little medium left – hence the lack of water retention. Fresh, well draining potting mix goes around the trimmed roots, firmed just enough to hold the plant upright. Then one good watering to settle everything, and that’s it for a while.

Step three: choosing where they “sleep”

In a small Tokyo apartment, space is the limiting factor, so the trick is to aim for dormancy rather than active growth. Peppers will slow right down if you keep them cool (about 10–15 °C), fairly dry, and in only modest light. A bright south facing window with warm air blasting from the heater is actually the worst place: they try to grow, can’t quite manage it, and become pest magnets.

What works better is a “meh” corner: an unheated greenhouse, a cool hallway, a spare room that sits just above outdoor temperatures. As long as the roots don’t freeze, peppers can happily sit there leafless for months. Many growers report success at anything from 5–12 °C as long as frost is excluded and watering is minimal. In central Tokyo, where winter nights usually hover around 1–5 °C and only occasionally dip below zero, a simple unheated greenhouse or sheltered roof nook with a bit of fleece on the coldest nights is often enough.

Watering is the main place people go wrong. Dormant peppers want the soil to dry out between drinks. Most sources suggest watering lightly every 2–4 weeks, just enough that the root ball never becomes bone dry dust, but never stays wet and cold either. If in doubt, don’t water yet.

Subscribe now

Waking them up on the balcony

In the 6 years I have lived here, Tokyo only tends to see any hint of snow in March; it’s as though summer is so hot that it takes all winter and the beginning of spring to cool completely. Sometime in March or early April, when Tokyo finally stops flirting with frost, you can start waking the peppers up. Allegedly, they’ll usually tell you they’re ready by pushing out tiny green buds along those bare stems. At that point:

• Move them into brighter light and slightly warmer conditions.

• Resume more regular watering.

• Give a gentle feed and, if you downsized them for winter, shift them back into a larger container with fresh mix.

You still need to harden them off before they go back to full rooftop life: a few days of moving pots in and out so they can adjust to wind and direct sun again. Once settled, overwintered plants usually flower weeks earlier than seedlings and can produce noticeably heavier crops.

And that’s basically it. Overwintering peppers on a high Tokyo balcony isn’t glamorous. It’s a bit of surgery, a bit of root taming, and then months of benign neglect in a cool corner. But if you’ve got a favorite variety, or a plant that really earned its place this year, it’s a surprisingly satisfying way to carry a little piece of summer across the coldest months and straight into next season.

Don’t take my word for it on a prized pepper, however; check back in March 2026 to see how mine survived!

title: Flower Seedling Timelapse
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
Just two days of basic GoPro timelapse on my seedling tray, growing borage, rudbeckia, aster, and thus-far ungerminated lovage and passion flower; in future I’ll try to capture from sprouting!

title: Beets in my Dreams
subtitle: Is it possible to grow good beets in a container?
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
I love beets. People love beets, or at least they should. This root has definitely grown on me as I’ve gotten older - childhood beets were nothing fantastic… I don’t remember them being served at home, and anywhere else they were usually canned and bland. Somewhere along the way, I was converted. The conversion moment is not a clear memory; I am sure it has a lot to do with years I spent in California and all the farmers markets I trawled for pretty veggies, although I can vaguely remember some Chicago kitchen experiments. As a gardener, however, I have dreamed of growing golden, chioggia, and bulls blood beets. My dreams look like this:

I know, I know: chioggia are disappointing, because the pretty striping fades when cooked - but that pattern is mesmerising.

Every Day in the Garden is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

This past year, I dedicated a rather large container to just beets, technically deep enough, but ended up with a disappointing harvest. Usually, I’d have lots of greens and not much root. This photo is fairly illustrative:

Damn, could I grow greens. Of course, I confused things a bit by interplanting beets and chard, but I really really tried with the beets. I truly did:

Well, doing a little research to be ready for next year, I think my two errors were too much nitrogen (LOOK at those greens) and inconsistent watering. I was all over the figurative map this year with irrigation, and I still am not super happy with my system, but hopefully I can figure that out before next season starts in earnest.

Common reasons for poor beet root production:

  • Excess nitrogen is the primary cause of abundant greens with no root development. Nitrogen promotes leafy growth while inhibiting bulb formation.

    • Yeah. I used fresh garden soil straight from the store, since it was only my second year in this garden. Definitely plenty of nitrogen in a fresh bag.

  • Poor soil conditions including compacted soil, insufficient depth, or heavy clay prevents proper root expansion.

    • Nope, I’m in a container with the aforementioned “fresh dirt” (I know it’s growing medium, not dirt, but come on…)

  • Inconsistent watering stresses plants and stops root development.

    • Yeah, I am surely guilty here. I promise to do better next year!

  • Overcrowding from failure to thin seedlings creates competition that stunts roots.

    • I don’t know about all that; other places I’ve read say beet and chard seeds (close relatives) are seed clusters, meaning several plants can grow from what looks like one seed. I’ll discuss this a little later.

  • Poor germination from cold soil temperatures, planting too deep, or old seeds means plants never establish properly.

    • I had good germination, my seeds were new, and I am not sure about the depth, so let’s look at that too.

What the cluster are you talking about, seedy?

What is commonly called a “beet seed” is actually a cluster containing multiple seeds (usually 2-5). This means each planted seed cluster can germinate into several seedlings close together.

Because of this, thinning is still critical. When the seedlings from a single seed cluster emerge, they grow very close to one another and compete for space, nutrients, and water. To encourage the growth of a healthy, well-formed root from one strong seedling, you need to thin out the extra plants within each cluster by snipping the weaker seedlings at soil level when they reach about 5 cm tall.

So, thinning does not mean removing entire seed clusters, but rather selectively reducing the number of seedlings that sprouted from each cluster to a single strong seedling per desired plant spot. This reduces overcrowding and allows sufficient root development, avoiding the issue of stunted or leafy-only beet plants. This practice is standard for beets and other vegetables with clustered seeds.

The minimum diameter of the “grow zone” (the space around a single beet plant) in a container to grow a nice, fat root is about 15 to 20 cm (6 to 8 inches). This space allows the beetroot enough room to expand properly without being crowded by neighboring plants or limited by container walls.

Providing at least 15 cm diameter per beet will help ensure good root development and prevent small or deformed roots caused by overcrowding or restricted growth. Larger spacing (up to 20 cm) is even better for bigger roots, but 15 cm is a minimum for healthy bulbs in containers.

If you have a container that is 30 cm wide (almost exactly a foot in diameter), you could grow two beets spaced 15 cm apart, each with adequate root zone space. I definitely won’t be competing with Dwight Schrute here on the roof terrace.

Nitty gritty deets for beets:

Container Selection

Choose containers that meet these specifications:

• Minimum depth: 25 cm (10 inches), though 30 cm (12 inches) is better for full-sized varieties

• Width: As large as practical; wider containers allow more plants

• Drainage: Essential drainage holes in the bottom

• Material: Food-safe materials (avoid treated wood)

Soil Mix Recipe

Create a loose, well-draining mix specifically for root vegetables. Use this formula:[13][14]

Basic Container Mix for Beets:

• 3 parts coconut coir or peat moss

• 2 parts compost (well-aged, not fresh manure)

• 1 part perlite

• 1 part vermiculite

• Optional: Add 2-3 tablespoons lime if using peat moss to adjust pH

Critical soil requirements:

• pH 6.0-7.0 (beets are sensitive to acidic soil)

• Loose texture to allow root expansion

• Well-draining but moisture-retentive

Avoid high-nitrogen amendments like fresh manure or blood meal, which cause leafy tops with poor roots.

Sunlight Requirements

Beets need 4-6 hours of direct sunlight daily minimum. They can tolerate partial shade but perform best with 6+ hours of full sun.

On your 5th-floor rooftop, ensure containers receive morning to midday sun if possible. Inadequate sunlight contributes to poor root development and leggy growth.

Watering Schedule

For containers, watering is more critical than in-ground growing:

Seedling stage (first 6 weeks):

• Keep top 10 cm (4 inches) of soil consistently moist

• Water when top 2.5 cm (1 inch) feels dry

• In containers, this typically means daily watering during warm weather, every 2-3 days in cooler conditions

Mature plants:

• Provide 2.5 cm (1 inch) of water per week total

• Water deeply but less frequently (2-3 times weekly)

• Container gardens dry faster than ground beds, especially on exposed rooftops

Critical: Maintain consistent moisture. Fluctuating wet-dry cycles cause cracked, misshapen roots and can stop root development entirely.

Fertilization for Root Development

Use fertilizers that promote roots, not leaves:

Recommended NPK ratios:

• 5-10-10 or 10-10-10 (balanced to low nitrogen)

• Avoid high-nitrogen fertilizers (like 20-10-10)

Application:

• Mix balanced granular fertilizer into soil at planting (follow package rates)

• Side-dress once during growth with phosphorus-rich fertilizer (like bone meal) to encourage root formation

• Phosphorus enhances root growth

• Potassium improves overall plant health and disease resistance

Troubleshooting Poor Germination

If seeds fail to emerge:

• Too cold: Soil below 10°C significantly delays germination

• Too deep: Seeds planted deeper than 2.5 cm may not emerge

• Too wet: Waterlogged soil causes seed rot

• Old seeds: Beet seeds remain viable 3-6 years if stored properly

• Pest damage: Inspect for cutworms or other soil pests

Growing Schedule Summary

1. Today: Soak seeds for 8-12 hours

2. Tomorrow: Plant 1 cm deep, 7-10 cm apart in prepared container mix

3. Days 5-21: Monitor for germination (expect 14-21 days at current Tokyo temperatures)

4. Week 2-3: Thin seedlings to one per spot when 5 cm tall

5. Weeks 4-8: Maintain consistent moisture, ensure adequate sun

6. Days 50-70: Begin harvesting when roots reach golf-ball size (5-7.5 cm diameter)

Claude made me a crude illustration of my favorite varietals:

_______

The very best beets I grew in 2025:

Beautiful greens, eh?

title: A Disappointing Potato Harvest
subtitle: I tried growing a fall crop of potatoes; one container at least was a bit of a bust!
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
This summer, I grew potatoes in a bag - a literal plastic bag of soil. I fell for the bait, a cute display in the nearby home store, with the potato soil bag planted right there in the outdoor section. Of course, rationally I knew that I can grow potatoes in any soil, as long as nutrients are supplied. But I just decided to fork over the two or so extra dollars, cause the bag said potato!

And with this 12 liter bag of soil, I bought two seed potatoes. I wasn’t expecting too much, but it’s worth a try, right? These two tiny seed potatoes produced a lot of foliage, and some very pretty flowers:

Every Day in the Garden is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

After that display, the foliage started looked a bit ragged, but still upright:

By July 4th, the foliage had died back, so it was time to put on my prospecting hat and sift for gold. Luckily my much better half recorded my gleeful reaction:

So that was fun, and fairly delicious. From what I gather, this is the only way to grow potatoes, as you other dirt farming fools have to gently fork through your actual ground patches, hoping that you don’t ruin a tender potato or even miss one to populate the bed with rogue potato growth.

As the summer heat died down, I put a few fancier seed potatoes into a 15 gallon grow bag with some “regular” soil - no more proprietary blends for this seasoned potato veteran. They grew along nicely for a few months (I should have recorded planting and such, but I have neither photo nor note) until it all flopped on the ground; last week it was basically done.

In the background there you can see healthier potatoes that I planted a couple of weeks later than these.

But they still look floppier than I think they should.

Well, something went wrong - not enough light? too little nutrients? Because the harvest was paltry:

And unlike the spring crop, there were definitely remains of the seed potatoes, looking somewhat slimy and rotten. Also, the damn grubs couldn’t have helped.

I think I’ll stick to sunny spring potatoes from now on.

This is all we got, and the big ones are split:
