title: Persephone Days
subtitle: Do you ever feel like the daylight is different this time of year?
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
When I first started growing things here in Tokyo, I thought this [comparatively] mild climate meant I could grow something all year. While it’s true, I can grow lots of hardy vegetables, I was perplexed last winter that things grew soooo… slowly…. At first, I noticed the shadow line of our building’s roof was much further out at midday than it had been in summer, so I moved everything to the edge to catch direct sun. That didn’t help much. Towards the end of winter I happened on a morsel in a gardening book I was reading (wish I could remember which) that mentioned the persephone days. Finally, a better explanation!

What’s this now?

Every Day in the Garden is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

The Persephone period is the time of year when natural daylight at a given location drops below 10 hours per day, triggering plant dormancy or sharply reduced growth. This concept was popularized by winter gardening expert Eliot Coleman and is rooted in Greek mythology, where Persephone’s annual descent into the underworld symbolizes the earth’s transition to winter dormancy.

According to ancient Greek myth, Persephone’s absence during her time in the underworld caused her mother Demeter, goddess of agriculture, to mourn, withholding growth from the earth until Persephone returned—a beautiful and poignant allegory for the seasonal dormancy of plants in winter.

Practical Gardening Use

In gardening, the Persephone period marks when daily sunlight falls below 10 hours, signaling that most plants will halt active growth or enter dormancy. Gardeners use this information to adjust planting schedules, avoid sowing seeds or transplanting tender seedlings, and plan for indoor seed starting before spring. The exact dates of the Persephone period depend on local latitude; for Tokyo, this usually runs from mid-November to late January, but latitude-specific calculation is recommended for precision.

Why It Matters

Understanding the Persephone period helps gardeners manage expectations for plant growth over winter, time indoor seed starting, and prepare for effective spring planting as sunlight increases again. Looks like I only have a couple of months to relax before I’m starting seedlings again. No worries - I’m quite ready to fire it up come February… maybe January 15!

title: Chrysanthemum Exhibition @ Shinjuku Gyoen
subtitle: Not my garden, but stunning flowers and technique!
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
Every year I seem to miss it, but today I was in the neigborhood and thought I’d go see the roses at Shinjuku Gyoen; while there I realized it was chrysanthemum time! I don’t have much history with these plants, but the way they’re treated here in Japan is fascinating.

The chrysanthemum exhibition at Shinjuku Gyoen is an annual autumn event with roots in imperial tradition, dating back to the late 19th century. Originally held for the Imperial Family at Akasaka Palace, it moved to Shinjuku Gyoen in 1929. The event features elaborate displays showcasing horticultural artistry—such as large-dome and cascading styles—that require almost a year of skilled cultivation. Today, it is open to the public and honors both Japanese garden aesthetics and the historical importance of chrysanthemums as imperial symbols.

Every Day in the Garden is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

Ichimonji and Kudamono-giku bed:

Large-flowered types are arranged in patterns resembling ceremonial horse bridles; Ichimonji has a single large bloom, while Kudamono has thin, tubular petals. Signs say this style was first created in 1925.

Edo-giku:

A classical Tokyo variety where each trained plant produces 27 flowers; the petals change their appearance as the flowers open. Signs explain this style was first created in 1925.

Ozukuri (“Thousand Bloom” Dome):

Hundreds of blossoms are created from a single plant by careful pruning and training, forming a large dome shape. From this under-skirt shot you can see that it really is just one stem:

This type was first displayed in 1884.

Ise, Choji, and Saga Varieties:

These beds feature:

  • Ise (from Mie): crinkled, drooping petals

  • Choji: anemone-like flowers

  • Saga (from Kyoto): thin, straight petals

Kengai (Cascade):

Small-flowered chrysanthemums are trained to drape downward like wild flowers on cliffs, producing a dramatic cascading effect

It was only the second day of the exhibition, so I’ll be curious to go back later and see how the flowers have opened and changed the displays!

History of the Exhibition:

The chrysanthemum exhibition at Shinjuku Gyoen has a long history that traces back to Japan’s imperial past. Chrysanthemums, or kiku, have been cherished in Japan for centuries as symbols of longevity, rejuvenation, and nobility. The flower is closely tied to the Imperial Family — the sixteen-petaled chrysanthemum crest adorns the Imperial Seal — and during the Meiji era, chrysanthemum cultivation was not merely a horticultural pursuit but a matter of national pride.

The tradition of public chrysanthemum displays began in the late 19th century. In 1878, an exhibition of chrysanthemums was first held in the gardens of the Akasaka Palace, then an Imperial property. These displays showcased sophisticated cultivation techniques and sought to elevate the art of chrysanthemum growing to an aesthetic form that blended imperial formality with nature’s grace. By 1929, the chrysanthemum exhibition had moved to Shinjuku Gyoen, which was itself originally an Imperial garden before opening to the public after World War II. The garden’s Japanese landscape — with its ponds, pavilions, and carefully composed scenery — provided the perfect setting for this seasonal celebration.

Throughout the prewar period, the chrysanthemum exhibition was managed under the auspices of the Imperial Household Agency. After the war, when Shinjuku Gyoen came under the jurisdiction of the Ministry of the Environment, the exhibition continued as a cherished autumn event, open to all visitors. The horticultural techniques on display — such as ōzukuri (large dome formations from a single stem) and kengai (cascading styles) — were preserved and passed down by master gardeners trained in imperial methods. Each display requires almost a year of careful pruning, pinching, and guiding to achieve the intricate shapes seen each November.

Today, the Shinjuku Gyoen chrysanthemum exhibition stands as both a public cultural event and a living link to Japan’s imperial garden traditions. It honors the meticulous artistry of chrysanthemum cultivation while inviting modern visitors to experience the refined seasonal beauty that was once reserved for the Imperial Court. Every year, the exhibition transforms the garden into a serene, floral tapestry — a reminder that Japan’s sense of beauty is rooted not only in fleeting blossoms like cherry trees, but also in the enduring patience and precision of the chrysanthemum.

Hundreds of blossoms are created from a single plant by careful pruning and training, forming a large dome shape. From this under-skirt shot you can see that it really is just one stem:
This type was first displayed in 1884.
Ise, Choji, and Saga Varieties:
These beds feature:
Ise (from Mie): crinkled, drooping petals
Choji: anemone-like flowers
Saga (from Kyoto): thin, straight petals
Kengai (Cascade):
Small-flowered chrysanthemums are trained to drape downward like wild flowers on cliffs, producing a dramatic cascading effect
It was only the second day of the exhibition, so I’ll be curious to go back later and see how the flowers have opened and changed the displays!
History of the Exhibition:

The chrysanthemum exhibition at Shinjuku Gyoen has along history that traces back to Japan’s imperial past. Chrysanthemums, orkiku, have been cherished in Japan for centuriesas symbols of longevity, rejuvenation, and nobility. The flower is closely tied to the Imperial Family — the sixteen-petaled chrysanthemum crest adorns the Imperial Seal — and during the Meiji era, chrysanthemum cultivation was not merely a horticultural pursuit but a matter of national pride.

The tradition of public chrysanthemum displays began in the late 19th century. In 1878, an exhibition of chrysanthemums was first held in the gardens of the Akasaka Palace, then an Imperial property. These displays showcased sophisticated cultivation techniques and sought to elevate the art of chrysanthemum growing to an aesthetic form that blended imperial formality with nature’s grace. By 1929, the chrysanthemum exhibition had moved to Shinjuku Gyoen, which was itself originally an Imperial garden before opening to the public after World War II. The garden’s Japanese landscape — with its ponds, pavilions, and carefully composed scenery — provided the perfect setting for this seasonal celebration.

Throughout the prewar period, the chrysanthemum exhibition was managed under the auspices of the Imperial Household Agency. After the war, when Shinjuku Gyoen came under the jurisdiction of the Ministry of the Environment, the exhibition continued as a cherished autumn event, open to all visitors. The horticultural techniques on display — such asōzukuri(large dome formations from a single stem) andkengai(cascading styles) — were preserved and passed down by master gardeners trained in imperial methods. Each display requires almost a year of careful pruning, pinching, and guiding to achieve the intricate shapes seen each November.

Today, the Shinjuku Gyoen chrysanthemum exhibition stands as both a public cultural event and a living link to Japan’s imperial garden traditions. It honors the meticulous artistry of chrysanthemum cultivation while inviting modern visitors to experience the refined seasonal beauty that was once reserved for the Imperial Court. Every year, the exhibition transforms the garden into a serene, floral tapestry — a reminder that Japan’s sense of beauty is rooted not only in fleeting blossoms like cherry trees, but also in the enduring patience and precision of the chrysanthemum.

title: Pretty peppers!
subtitle: What a lovely year for sweet peppers in my containers
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
I have a fairly lame history with growing peppers; they seem to requiree a lot of work I didn’t put in. The seeds were slow to germinate, the plants looked ugly, peppers were small and I wasn’t sure when to pick… of course, all that is just operator error.

This year, I had much more success:

On the left is “korean” pepper that I got from a wonderful garden store. This plant grew huge, and has been pumping beautiful peppers all summer long. The middle is from seed I saved from a long sweet pepper; today I decided to pick some at this very light yellow stage to see what they’ll be like. The right are purple Japanese peppers that eventually turn red if left on. This plant in particular is beautiful, with speckled leaves and gorgeous flowers:

I’m now researching how to overwinter pepper plants. Did you know they’re perennial? Stay tuned… more on that later!

title: Incredible Okra
subtitle: It's not everyone's favorite vegetable, but it's growing on me!
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
Another unexpected fun fact about living in Japan: Okra is popular here!

I cut down two or three mature okra plants about two months ago, but haven’t gotten around to throwing the material in the compost because I need to chop it up and it’s THIIICKKKK. Today during some cleaning I noticed it was still green, and seemingly growing leaves:

Every Day in the Garden is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

Today I learned: It is uncommon, but not impossible for mature okra stems left after cutting to show signs of regrowth, such as sprouting new leaves or even forming pods, especially if parts of the root system were left attached or intact. Okra is known to respond to “topping” or pruning by producing side branches and new leaves from dormant buds lower on the stem, provided some root is present and the stem wasn’t entirely severed from its base. What I was observing—tiny bright green leaves emerging from completely severed okra stems—is unusual but can happen temporarily as a survival response. When okra stems are cut off from the main root system and left with no root access, they can sometimes activate dormant buds and sprout small shoots or leaves using the energy stored in their tissues. How crazy is that?

One of the pods was dried, and so I cracked it open to reveal several seeds - guess I don’t need to buy any more!

Just for fun, I got some “red burgundy” okra seeds from the US. They make beautiful plants! And crazy deep-red okra pods which sadly lose color when cooked.

These things are practically trees; look at the thickness of that trunk! Almost as thick as my wrist:

Why is okra popular in Japan?

Okra became popular in Japan due to a combination of historical introduction and a growing appreciation for its unique texture and health benefits. The plant was first introduced in the late Edo period (1603–1868) or early Meiji period (1868–1912), likely brought via America after its African origins. Early Japanese adoption focused mainly on the ornamental value of okra flowers, and it took until the 1960s–1970s for okra to gain widespread culinary popularity.

Okra fits the Japanese love of “neba neba” (sticky, slimy) foods, a category that includes other traditional ingredients like natto and yamaimo. Its unique mucilaginous texture made it especially appealing for hot summer months, when these textures are thought to help with stamina and combating heat fatigue. Nutritional value promoted okra as a health food; it is recognized for dietary fiber, vitamins, and minerals, which led to its labeling as a “stamina vegetable” in post-war Japanese cuisine. Culinary adaptation included creative uses in salads, soups, tempura, and as an accompaniment for rice and noodles, making okra a summer staple across Japan.

Okra’s Japanese name “amerikā neri” or simply “okura” reflects its foreign origins, but today it is fully embraced as a local summer favorite.

title: Fava planting time!
subtitle: In regions with mild winters, planting in fall for a spring harvest can be effective
author: balconybotanybeyond
raw_article:
I did not grow up with fave beans. Perhaps my first exposure was quite indirect, via the Silence of the Lambs:

A census taker once tried to test me. I ate his liver with some fava beans and a nice Chianti

However, they’re frequently mentioned on my favorite BBC gardening program and quite popular (and expensive in grocery stores) in Japan, so last year I gave them a try. A lot of information I saw/read about them said they’re aphid magnets, so “pinch out the tender tips” to avoid that. I still have no idea what that means, so I didn’t do it. I ended up with an aphid farm:

Every Day in the Garden is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

But also, as you can see from that video, lots of things came that like to eat aphids. My harvest was really pretty good, for the minimal effort I put in:

So what is a fava?

Fava beans (Vicia faba), also known as broad beans, are one of the oldest cultivated crops in the world, with evidence of their domestication dating back over 10,000 years in the eastern Mediterranean and the Near East, especially regions like the Galilee in northern Israel. Their cultivation predates grains in some ancient societies, making them an important protein source before cereals became widespread.​

Origins and Historical Context

Fava beans most likely originated in the eastern Mediterranean/Near East, spreading to Egypt, North Africa, China, and throughout European agriculture. Ancient Greeks and Romans used them in rituals and voting, and they were a dietary staple for early civilizations due to their resilience and nutritional content.​

Differences from Other Legumes

Fava beans differ from peas in both flavor and texture; they are larger, flatter, and described as buttery and earthy, while peas are smaller and have a sweeter flavor.​

Compared to white beans, lima beans, and kidney beans, fava beans have a distinct appearance (larger, flat, pale green), and their nutrition profile is richer in copper, phosphorus, vitamin B2, and folate, while lima beans contain more fiber, iron, and vitamin B1.​

Fava beans thrive in cooler weather and can be planted as a winter crop in some regions, distinguishing them ecologically from other commonly grown beans like Phaseolus species (kidney, pinto, and black beans), which prefer warmer conditions. They grow best in temperatures between 60°F to 65°F (15.5°C to 18.3°C). This temperature range provides ideal conditions for healthy growth and robust yields. The plants prefer cool, moist soil and can even tolerate light frost, making them suitable for early spring planting in many regions. When temperatures rise above 80°F (26.7°C), fava beans experience several negative effects; the overall quality of the beans decreases, heat stress leads to a significant reduction in bean production, higher temperatures make the plants more susceptible to pest infestations, and sustained temperatures above 80°F can cause the flowers to become sterile, preventing pod formation.

And now, a table for all you bean queens:
